Note: I removed the old build-up intro (the `playBass`/`bassPattern`/`leadNotes` sequences) so this arrangement plays first, as you described. If you'd rather keep that intro in front of it, say the word — it's still in my history and I can put it back.

The key detail: `waitForBeat` uses absolute beat numbers, so the arrangement's waits must continue from beat 64 (where the intro ends), and I'll put `loopHere()` right after the intro so the intro plays once and the groove loops.Done — compiled and playing. Structure now:

- **Intro (beats 0–64, plays once):** the original build-up — bass+lead, then +drums, then +pad, twice through.
- **`loopHere()`** right after the intro, so from here on it loops.
- **A × 2 (beats 64–80, 80–96):** bass + chords + pad.
- **B × 2 (beats 96–112, 112–128):** the same, plus drums + lead.

Each loop replays A,A,B,B while the intro stays a one-time lead-in. If you'd rather have the whole thing (intro included) loop, just say so and I'll move `loopHere()` to the top.

loopHere loops back to the beginning of the song. I moved it to the end of the song. Where you had it now prevented the last part from playing. Just a note - nothing you need to do right now, but we should improve the agent instructions on this.

Got it, thanks for the correction — noted for how I'll handle it going forward:

- **`loopHere()` = "loop back to the START of the song from this point."** It's the *end* marker (where playback jumps back to the beginning), not a loop-return target. So it belongs at the very end of the song.
- Placing it mid-song (like right after the intro) cuts off everything after it — which is why my last section never played.

No changes from me right now. And agreed the agent instructions describe it ambiguously ("marks the current position as a loop point") — that phrasing led me to treat it as a loop-return anchor rather than an end-of-song marker. Worth tightening.
I replaced with waitDuration ( which now works ) instead of waitForBeat

Makes sense — `waitDuration(16)` is the better fit here since each section is a relative 16‑beat block. It sidesteps the absolute-beat bookkeeping entirely: no more computing 80 / 96 / 112 / 128, and inserting or reordering sections won't throw the timing off. Good to know it's working now.

I'll use `waitDuration` for relative advances like this going forward. Nothing needed from me — just say the word when you want to keep building.

Yes, absolutely — an electric/Hammond-style organ is a great fit for Faust (additive drawbars + a little vibrato and key-click). Let me first look at how the current instruments are wired so I add the organ correctly.The synth uses a shared jpverb reverb send — each instrument is a `ReverbSendChannel`. I'll add the organ as channel 6, wired the same way. First, let me author the organ in Faust — a drawbar (tonewheel) organ with a bit of vibrato/chorus and key-click for that electric character:Organ transpiled cleanly (class `Organ`). Now wiring it into the synth as channel 6, using the same `ReverbSendChannel` pattern as the others:Now let me look at the current song (you've edited it since — moved `loopHere`, switched to `waitDuration`) so I route the first pad section to the organ correctly:Organ is wired into the synth. Now I'll add it to the song: register it as channel 6, make an organ track, and route the **first** pad section (sequence 3) to play `padNotes` on the organ instead of the padsynth.Compiled and playing. Here's what I did:

**New Faust instrument — `faust/organ.dsp`** (electric/tonewheel organ):
- Additive drawbars at Hammond ratios (16′, 8′, 5⅓′, 4′, 2⅔′, 2′)
- Gentle scanner-style pitch vibrato for the chorusy electric character
- A key-click transient on note onset
- Mild Leslie-style tremolo
- Organ-style envelope (instant on, full sustain, short release)

**Synth:** added `Organ` as channel 6 (a `ReverbSendChannel` with a 0.35 reverb send, like the others).

**Song:** registered `addInstrument('organ')` as channel 6, added an `organ` track, and routed the **first** pad section (sequence 3 of the intro) to play `padNotes` on the organ instead of the padsynth. The second intro pad and the main-loop pad are untouched.

One note on interpretation: I took "the first padNotes section" as the first of the two intro `playPad` calls. If you actually meant *both* intro pad sections (i.e. all of `padNotes`) should be the organ, say the word and I'll switch the second one too. And if you want to tweak the drawbar registration (brighter, more sub, etc.), that's a quick edit.
I meant both intro pad sections, and we need to transpose it one octave up to work nice with the organ.

Two changes: route the **second** intro pad section to the organ as well, and transpose the organ part up one octave. Since `padNotes` now feeds only the organ, I'll bump its octaves directly (5 → 6).Compiled and playing. Both intro pad sections (sequences 3 and 4) now play on the organ, and `padNotes` is transposed up one octave (5 → 6) so it sits nicely in the organ's register. The `playPad`/padsynth path is left intact but unused in the intro; the main-loop `recPad` is untouched.
are you able to make a new lead based on the bass? it should have the same characteristics as the bass when playing at mid/high range notes on the midi keyboard, but it should be a bit fatter/richer.

Let me read the current bass Faust source so the new lead shares its character.The bass is a sub sine + detuned saw pair through an envelope-swept lowpass (cutoff opens to ~1.1 kHz). For a lead I'll keep that DNA but make it fatter (a unison saw stack + a sub-octave) and let the filter cutoff track pitch so mid/high notes keep the bass character instead of going dull.Transpiled cleanly (class `Basslead`). Now I'll wire it into the synth as channel 7:The import was added; the channel line didn't match. Let me find the exact anchor.The user added an organ volume line. Let me anchor after it:Now register it in the song as channel 7 so the channel count stays in sync with the synth and it's addressable:Done — compiled OK. The new lead is ready as **channel 7** (`basslead`).

**`faust/basslead.dsp`** keeps the bass's DNA — sub sine + detuned saws through an envelope-swept lowpass, same ADSR — but is fatter/richer:
- 7-voice detuned unison saw stack (vs. the bass's 2)
- an added sub-octave sine for extra body
- cutoff now tracks pitch (`freq*3 + 300 + env*1500`) so mid/high keyboard notes stay bright and keep the bass character instead of going dull like the bass filter would up there

**Synth:** wired as `midichannels[7]`, a `ReverbSendChannel` with a 0.25 reverb send. **Song:** registered `addInstrument('basslead')` as channel 7 so the indexing stays in sync.

Play it live on MIDI **channel 8** (channel index 7). If you want it brighter/fatter or with more/less detune spread, that's a one-line tweak. Want me to dial anything in, or use it somewhere in the song?
that's good. Can you also make it so that the reverbsendchannel, also respects the channel volume for what it sends to the reverb?

Let me check how `MidiChannel` handles volume and the signal, so I scale the reverb send by the right field.Found it. The order is: `preprocess()` runs (line 320) **before** volume/pan are applied (lines 322-323), so the `ReverbSendChannel` currently sends the pre-fader signal — ignoring channel volume. `volume` is a field on `MidiChannel` (set by CC7), so I just multiply the send by `this.volume`:Done — compiled OK. The `ReverbSendChannel.preprocess()` now scales its send by `this.volume`, so the amount each channel feeds into the shared jpverb tracks its CC7 channel volume (just like the dry signal does in the core mixer). Lowering a channel's volume now pulls its reverb down proportionally too — e.g. your organ's `controlchange(7, 40)` will send correspondingly less to the reverb.

Note I only applied volume (not pan), since that's what you asked. Say the word if you'd also want the send to follow the channel pan.
which control change number is mapped to the faust hslider gain?

Good question — let me check how the transpiler handles the standard `gain` control versus the CC-mapped params.Short answer: **none** — `gain` isn't mapped to a CC at all.

In the faust2as transpiler, `freq`, `gate`, and `gain` are treated as the standard per-voice Faust MIDI-polyphony controls and are explicitly excluded from CC assignment (they're in the `excludedFields` set). Instead:

- **`gain` ← note velocity.** In the generated voice's `noteon()`: `this.gain = <f32>velocity / 127.0`. So it's driven per note by how hard you play, not by a controller.
- `freq` ← note number, `gate` ← note on/off.

Only your *extra* hsliders/nentries (cutoff, detune, etc. — anything beyond freq/gate/gain) get CC numbers, assigned sequentially from **CC 0** upward, skipping the reserved ones (7=volume, 10=pan, 64=sustain, 91=reverb). If there are too many params for the safe CC range, it switches the whole instrument to NRPN (CC 99/98/6) instead.

So if your goal is to ride the *level* of a channel via a controller, the lever is the channel **volume on CC 7** (`midichannels[n].controlchange(7, value)` → `this.volume`) — which, after the last change, now also scales that channel's reverb send. The per-voice `gain` stays purely velocity-driven.

Yes, I understand — you want subtle "grace"/ghost pick-up notes at ~1/4 velocity landing just *before* certain main hits, occasionally (not every beat), and staggered so the kick, hi-hat and bass ghosts never fire at the same instant. That's a nice humanizing variation.
